title: Externalities
description: Practice what you've learned about positive externalities and negative externalities in this exercise.
topic_path: Market failure and the role of government

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the marginal social cost $(MSC)$, marginal social benefit $(MSB)$, and marginal private benefit $(MPB)$ of a good that is sold in a perfectly competitive market.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What kind of externality is this, and what area represents the deadweight loss generated if this market is unregulated?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph illustrates the relationship between Price and Quantity with curves representing Marginal Social Cost (S equals M S C), Marginal Social Benefit (M S B), and Marginal Private Benefit (D equals M P B). The y-axis is labeled Price, and the x-axis is labeled Quantity. The upward-sloping S equals M S C curve intersects the downward-sloping M S B curve at point Y (Q subscript 1, P subscript a) and the downward-sloping D equals M P B curve at point X (Q subscript 2, P subscript c). W is a point on the M S B curve at (Q subscript 2, P subscript a), and Z is a point on the D equals M P B curve at (Q subscript 1, and P subscript d). Horizontal dashed lines connect the prices P subscript a, P subscript b, P subscript c, and P subscript d to their respective points on the curves.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private benefit (MPB), and the marginal social benefit (MPB) associated with the market for snizzles.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/99f15eeabda82004e69f2e67f3a816bde5ed180c)

**Which of the following best describes what happens if the government imposes a price floor of $\$14$?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The externality is internalized by buyers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following correctly shades the area that represents the deadweight loss associated with a positive externality?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ![A graph represents a market model with supply and demand curves indicating price on the Y-axis and quantity on the X-axis. A third curve, labeled "M S B," lies above the demand curve and starts at a higher price on the Y-axis, sloping downward to the bottom right. The supply curve "M S C" intersects "M S B" at points (10, 14) and "M P B" at point (8, 12). A triangular area bounded by (8, 12), (8, 16), and (10, 14) is shaded. assessment_items.question_content:
The marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private cost (MPC), and marginal social benefit (MSB) associated with the production of a good are shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ef2acf24cfd807071788eb399cb28c8e0a083222)

**If this market is unregulated, what area represents deadweight loss?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private cost (MPC), and marginal social benefit (MSB) associated with the production of a good are shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a4823e13bfe217a23fdef96b1dc342bd03056930)

**What is the socially optimal price and quantity in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The graph shown here illustrates the marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private cost (MPC), demand (D), and marginal revenue (MR) for the only firm producing plastic in a market.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0475b56b8a578414f37ea1e036c37db5dbb20d0e)

**What is the socially optimal quantity and price?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following graphs has the deadweight loss associated with a negative externality correctly shaded?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ![A graph represents a market model with supply and demand curves indicating price on the Y-axis and quantity on the X-axis. The supply curve, labeled "M S C," starts from the bottom left and slopes upward to the top right. The demand curve, labeled "M S B," starts from the top left and slopes downward to the bottom right. A third curve, labeled "M P C," lies below the supply curve and slopes upward to the top right. The supply curve "M S C" intersects "M S B" at point "A" and "M P C" at point "D." Dashed lines connect point "A" horizontally to price level P x and vertically to quantity Q a, and point "D," where "M P C" intersects "M S B," horizontally to price level P y and vertically to quantity Q b. Dashed lines also extend from price levels P w, and Q b meet at C, P z and Q a meet at B. assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private benefit (MPB), and the marginal social benefit (MPB) associated with the market for snizzles.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/99f15eeabda82004e69f2e67f3a816bde5ed180c)

**If the government wants to intervene in this market, which of the following policies most likely leads to the optimal quantity being sold in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A per-unit subsidy of $\$4$ per unit.
A per-unit tax of $\$4$ per unit.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the market for flazzles, including the marginal social benefit (MSB), marginal social cost (MSC), and marginal private benefit (MPB).

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9c9d15e811d34fa2020341d327969b8c3d38012)

**If a government wants to correct the externality shown in this market, which of the following would be the best choice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A per unit subsidy of $\$8$ to buyers
A per unit tax on buyers of $\$16$
A per unit tax on sellers of $\$22$

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows a perfectly competitive unregulated market for books with marginal social cost (MSC), marginal social benefit (MSB), and marginal private benefit (MPB).

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e6d9520b60b87bb64f58504698156a04f71d6d5)

**Which of the following statements is true based on this graph?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Buyers of books could be subsidized to generate the socially efficient quantity
Consumption of books has a negative externality
A binding price ceiling should be imposed on this market to generate the socially efficient quantity.
Sellers should be taxed to generate the socially efficient quantity.
The quantity sold in this market is socially optimal

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the marginal social cost $(MSC)$, marginal social benefit $(MSB)$, and marginal private benefit $(MPB)$ of a good that is sold in a perfectly competitive market.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba0a665185e137a7a570dba957e3df4c1e19216c)

**A government could correct this externality by:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A lump sum tax of $P_b - P_d$

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the market for vaccines, including the marginal social benefit (MSB), marginal social cost (MSC), and marginal private benefit (MPB).

[[☃ image 1]]

**The government can attempt to correct this externality by setting**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure presents a graph of three lines in the first quadrant of a coordinate plane. The horizontal axis is labeled “Quantity” and the vertical axis is labeled “Price.”

There are three quantities on the horizontal axis. The quantity Q 2 appears about halfway across the horizontal axis. The quantity Q 1 is between the origin and Q 2 but is closer to Q 2 than the origin. The quantity Q 3 is about three fourths of the way across the horizontal axis. Five prices appear along the vertical axis. From bottom to top the prices are P A, P B, P C, P D, and P E. The distance between P A and the origin is larger than the distance between P A, P B, P C, P D, and P E and P A, P B, P C, P D, and P E are an equal distance apart. 

There are five points on the graph. The first point is at coordinate Q 1 comma P E and is labeled V. The second point is at coordinate Q 2 comma P D. and is labeled W. The third point is at coordinate Q 2 comma P B and is labeled X. The fourth point is at coordinate Q 3 comma P C and is labeled Y. The fifth point is at coordinate Q 3 comma P A and is labeled Z.

A straight line labeled M S B starts at the vertical axis near the top of the vertical axis and goes down and to the right and passes first through point Q 1 comma P E, then through point Q 2 comma P D, and finally through point Q 3 comma P E. The second straight line labeled M P B starts at the vertical axis slightly below M S B and is parallel to M S B, and passes first through Q 2 comma P B, and then through Q 3 comma P A. The third straight line is labeled M S C and starts at the vertical axis slightly above the origin and goes up and to the right, and passes first through  Q 2 comma P B where it intersects the curve M P B and then through point Q 3 comma P C where it intersects the curve labeled M S B.

choices.content: a per unit tax of $P_b - P_c$.
a per unit tax of Pb - Pd.

assessment_items.question_content:
The marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private cost (MPC), and marginal social benefit (MSB) associated with the production of a good are shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ef2acf24cfd807071788eb399cb28c8e0a083222)

**If the government wants to intervene in this market to eliminate deadweight loss, which of the following is the best choice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a per-unit tax on sellers of $\$12$

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the marginal social cost (MSC), marginal private cost (MPC), and marginal social benefit (MSB) in the market for kablams.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18fd54a3b96e3fd0714644fd6895cdefa412f1e6)

**Which of the following would most likely eliminate the deadweight loss associated with production of kablams?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A per unit tax of $P_x - P_z$
A per unit subsidy of $P_x - P_z$
A lump sum tax of $P_x - P_z$
A lump sum tax of $(P_x - P_z) \times (Q_b - Q_a)$
A per unit tax of $P_w - P_z$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following would be most effective at reducing the deadweight loss associated with a negative externality?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A per unit tax equal to the external cost of production.
A per unit subsidy equal to the external cost of production
A lump sum tax equal to the external cost
A lump sum subsidy equal to the external cost.
A per unit subsidy to buyers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes why lump-sum taxes on producers don't eliminate deadweight loss in the short run in a market with a negative externality?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They don't reduce a firm's profit.
They are too difficult to impose.
They change a firm's choice of output.
They alter the demand for the good.
